coredata.dc:title: High photoluminescent carbon nanodots and quercetin-Al3+ construct a ratiometric fluorescent sensing system
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Yu
Yan, Fanyong
Dai, Linfeng
Luo, Yunmei
Fu, Yang
Yang, Ning
Wun, Jingyun
Chen, Li
coredata.dc:description: Highly blue fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) were synthesized by the hydrothermal method from citric acid and diethylenetriamine with the quantum yield of about ∼86%. Quercetin (QCT) and its fluorescent metal-ion complex (QCT-Al3+) can efficiently coordinate on the surface of CNDs, the emission of CNDs at 429nm completely overlapped with the absorption peak of QCT-Al3+ at 428nm. Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Al3+ was developed based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) with CNDs as donor and QCT-Al3+ as acceptor. A linear calibration of F 429/F 481 versus aluminum ion concentration was obtained within 1–20 and 20–60μM, along with a detection limit of 558nM. Moreover, the ESIPT–FRET ratiometric fluorescence system based on the CNDs/QCT-Al3+ was applied for fluorescent test papers with satisfying resolution.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The establishment of ratiometric fluorescent sensing system based on ESIPT–FRET

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration for Al3+ sensing based on the CNDs/QCT-Al3+ ESIPT–FRET ratiometric fluorescence system. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) TEM images, (b) IR spectrum, (c) XPS survey scan, (d) C1s, (e) N1s, (f) O1s XPS spectrum of CNDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–vis spectrum (red line) and fluorescence spectrum (blue line) of the CNDs aqueous solution. (b) Fluorescence spectra (excitation wavelength from 320 to 380nm in 20nm increments) of CNDs dispersions. Inset: the normalized spectrum. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Printed patterns obtained by CNDs ink, (b) bright-field transmission image and (c) fluorescence image of NIH-3T3 cells incubated with 20μgmL−1 CNDs for 30min at 37°C. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The fluorescence (solid lines) and absorption (dashed lines) spectra of CNDs (red), QCT (black) and QCT-Al3+ conjugate (blue). (b) Fluorescence spectra of the CNDs/QCT ESIPT–FRET assay system in the presence of various amounts of Al3+ (0, 2, 4, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 and 80μM). (c) The linear calibration graph between F 429/F 481 and the Al3+ concentration. CNDs: 2μgmL−1; QCT: 10μM. (λ ex =360nm). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The fluorescence spectra of the CNDs/QCT ESIPT–FRET sensing system upon addition of 20μM of various metal ions. (b) The variation of fluorescence intensity ratio of lg(F 429/F 481) for the CNDs/QCT ESIPT–FRET sensing system in the presence of various metal ions. (c) Fluorescence intensity ratios of F 429/F 481 for the CNDs/QCT ESIPT–FRET sensing system in the presence of 20μM Al3+ and with the addition of 20μM different anions respectively. (λ ex =360nm). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Printed patterns obtained by (a) CNDs, (b) CNDs/QCT and (c) CNDs/QCT-Al3+ solutions. (d) Fluorescence changes for CNDs/QCT ratiometric fluorescence detection system: addition of small amount of Al3+ decreased the fluorescence intensity of CNDs; the fluorescence of QCT-Al3+ obtained when excess of Al3+ was added; and fluorescence recovery as result of EDTA solution addition (1eq. of Al3+). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The solvent is ethanol, pH maintained at neutral.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The color characteristics of the CD/QCT impregnated fluorescent test paper sensing different concentrations of Al3+.

head.ce:title: High photoluminescent carbon nanodots and quercetin-Al3+ construct a ratiometric fluorescent sensing system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Hollow Fiber Membrane Materials and Processes, Key Lab of Fiber Modification & Functional Fiber of Tianjin, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Hollow Fiber Membrane Materials and Processes
Key Lab of Fiber Modification & Functional Fiber of Tianjin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly blue fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) were synthesized by the hydrothermal method from citric acid and diethylenetriamine with the quantum yield of about ∼86%. Quercetin (QCT) and its fluorescent metal-ion complex (QCT-Al3+) can efficiently coordinate on the surface of CNDs, the emission of CNDs at 429nm completely overlapped with the absorption peak of QCT-Al3+ at 428nm. Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Al3+ was developed based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) with CNDs as donor and QCT-Al3+ as acceptor. A linear calibration of F 429/F 481 versus aluminum ion concentration was obtained within 1–20 and 20–60μM, along with a detection limit of 558nM. Moreover, the ESIPT–FRET ratiometric fluorescence system based on the CNDs/QCT-Al3+ was applied for fluorescent test papers with satisfying resolution.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) are the most popular carbon-based nanomaterials in recent years and have successfully overcome some defects of traditional semiconductor quantum dots [1–6]. It is highly evaluated not only for the excellent optical performance and small size effect, but also for the great biocompatibility and ease to achieve surface functionalization [7,8]. Therefore, CNDs are promising as substitutes for semiconductor quantum dots or organic dyes for preparation of fluorescent nanosensors.

Due to their high fluorescent emission and broad excitation spectra, CNDs are excellent donors for fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system [9–11]. FRET attracts a considerable attention because of its simpleness of building ratiometric fluorescent systems [12–19]. Despite the numerous applications of FRET reported, it remains an underappreciated and under-developed analytical tool [20–23]. The two key issues in designing effective FRET-based sensing systems are the suitable fluorescent donor and the precise spatial immobilization of the fluorophores [24]. Traditionally, in spatial arrangement of FRET, the conventional design connected two fluorophores by a spacer through covalent links. However, such fluorescent sensors are difficult to be prepared and still suffer from photobleaching and interference [25,26].

In this paper, we report a simple, aqueous based synthesis approach preparing CNDs by using citric acid with diethylenetriamine. The quantum yield of the CNDs is as high as 86%, which is the highest value reported for fluorescent nanomaterials, and is almost equal to fluorescent dyes. The fluorescence of CNDs was found that it can be efficiently quenched by quercetin (3,3′,4′,5,7-pentahydroxyflavone, QCT), one of the most common flavonols present in nature with attractive anticancer, antiviral, antimutagenic and lipid peroxidation inhibitory effects [27–31]. Particular attention was paid to the complexation of QCT with Al3+ due to its excellent fluorescence properties by the inhibition of excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) process [32]. The emission of CNDs completely overlapped with the absorption peak of QCT-Al3+. Thus a new strategy for constructing an ESIPT–FRET ratiometric fluorescence system is developed with CNDs as donor and QCT-Al3+ conjugate as acceptor. In this design, covalent immobilization of the probe molecules is not required, and CNDs were achieved with minimal probe modification. In Al3+ detection or imaging, this ESIPT–FRET sensing system possesses both favorable sensitivity and selectivity [33,34]. It was also applied to fluorescent test paper for Al3+ ions and fluorescent ink with satisfying resolution (Fig. 1 ).

Citric acid anhydrous, diethylenetriamine, toluene and ethanol purchased from the local company. Quinine sulfate (99%, suitable for fluorescence) and quercetin were supplied by Aladdin. All other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. The solutions of metal ions were prepared from Al(NO3)3·9H2O, NaNO3, Cd(NO3)2·2H2O, Mn(NO3)2·4H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, KNO3, La(NO3)3·6H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Ca(NO3)2, AgNO3, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Ba(NO3)2 and Zn(NO3)2·6H2O.

The IR spectra were recorded on a Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (TENSOR37). The XPS spectra measurements were performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (EDAX, GENESIS 60S). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed on an electronic microscope (HITACHI, H-7650). The absorption spectra of the samples were measured on a UV–vis spectrometer (Purkinje, General TU-1901). Fluorescence spectra were obtained by an F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer with the excitation wavelength of 360nm. Particle sizes were measured on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90. The fluorescence imaging was observed under an inverted fluorescence microscope with a 20× objective lens (Olympus, IX71). The color characteristics were obtained by a computer color measurement instrument (Datacolor, SF-600 plus). Phosphate buffer is obtained by mixing appropriate amount of 0.2M Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 solutions, and the pH value of the buffer is regulated with an Orion 818 pH meter (Thermo Fisher Scientific). The deionized water obtained from a Millipore NanoPure purification system (resistivity higher than 18.2MΩcm−1).

Citric acid anhydrous (0.42g) and diethylenetriamine (911μL) were dissolved in deionized water (10mL) and heated at 200°C for 5h in a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) autoclave (15mL). Then, sodium hydroxide was added into the brown-black and transparent solution to adjust the pH to 14. The solid was obtained by rotary evaporation and dissolved in ethanol. After removing the insoluble precipitate by centrifugation, the final product was obtained by rotary evaporation and washed by methylbenzene in order to purify the CNDs.

A 1.0mM stock solution of QCT was prepared in absolute ethanol, and CNDs were dissolved in double distilled water to afford 1.0mgmL− 1 aqueous solution. 20μL of the CND solution and 100μL of the QCT solution were added to 10mL volumetric flasks, then Al3+ solution ranging from 0 to 1000μL were added and constant volume with ethanol. All samples were prepared at room temperature. Fluorescence measurements were carried out with excitation and mission slit width of 10 and 5nm and excitation wavelength was 360nm.

NIH-3T3 cells were cultured in (Invitrogen) supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS, Invitrogen) in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 at 37°C. For imaging, the cells were seeded on a coverslip in 6-well plate 12h before use. The culture medium was replaced by 2.5mL fresh medium containing 2mgmL−1 CNDs and the cells were incubated for another 30min. After being washed with fresh 1×PBS (pH=7.0) three times, the cells were imaged on an inverted fluorescence microscope. The excitation wavelength was 360nm, and the fluorescence emission between 400 and 500nm was collected for cell imaging.

To evaluate cytotoxicity of CNDs was taken as an example to perform a MTT assay on NIH-3T3 cells with CND concentrations from 0 to 200μgmL−1. A cytotoxicity screen (Fig. S1) was conducted for four CND concentrations: 0, 50, 100, 200μgmL−1. The cellular viability estimated was ca. 96% in 24h after treatment with 200μgmL−1 of CNDs, exhibiting low toxicity to NIH-3T3 cells.

The amino-functionalized CNDs shown in the TEM images are monodisperse nanoparticles of near spherical morphology distributed within the range of 1–4nm in size and the average diameter is 2.51nm (Fig. 2 a, Fig. S2). IR spectrum was used to identify the functional groups present on carbon nanodots. The peaks at 3410cm−1 and 2979cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of –NH– and C–H. Two characteristic peaks at 1654 and 1579cm−1 assigning to the vibrations of CO and N–H, respectively, suggested the successful surface passivation reaction of CNDs (Fig. 2b). Moreover, the surface groups were also investigated by XPS analysis. The three peaks at 284.9, 399.4, 531.4eV shown in the XPS spectrum of CNDs (Fig. 2c) can be attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The C1s XPS spectrum of CNDs (Fig. 2d) reveals three peaks at 284.7, 285.9 and 288.2eV, which can be attributed to C–C, C–N and CO bonding respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) exhibits three peaks at 399.1, 400.0 and 401.3eV, which are associated with C–N–C, N–(C)3 and N–H groups, respectively. The two peaks at 531.0 and 532.0eV in O1s spectrum (Fig. 2f) are attributed to CO and C–OH/C–O–C groups, respectively. The spectrum in the UV–vis absorption (Fig. 3 a) region exhibits a peak at around 350nm and another absorption band at 240nm attributable to the π=π∗ transition of the aromatic sp2 domains [35]. In the 1H NMR spectrum and the 13C NMR spectrum, sp2 carbons were also detected (Fig. S3). The peak centered about 345nm due to the trapping of excited state energy by the surface states leads to strong emission at 431nm (Fig. 3b) [36–38]. The fluorescence quantum yield was as high as ca. 86% (Fig. S4). This strong emission can be attributable to presence of hetero atoms of CNDs: The doped nitrogen enhances the emission of the CNDs by inducing an upward shift of the Fermi level and electrons in the conduction band [39]. Moreover, the nitrogen bonding to carbon disorders the carbon hexagonal rings and creates emission energy traps for the particles through the radiative recombination induced by electron–hole pairs [40–43]. On the other hand, the oxidation level of CNDs plays an important role in quantum yield [44]. sp2 and sp3 carbon atoms function as fluorescent centers, while the decreased sp2 carbon level in oxidized CNDs depresses the radiative recombination of excitons for fluorescent emission. Since the present strategy did not require strong acids and oxidants, a lower level of oxidation could further improve the fluorescent emission from CNDs.

Since CNDs have shown the excellent photoluminescence characters, CNDs were used for printing versatile patterns of fluorescent ink and bioimaging study of CNDs using NIH-3T3 cell. Through screen printing, a bright blue fluorescence pattern emerged under the ultraviolet light (Fig. 4 a). In order to know the cell imaging application and biocompatibility of the CNDs, we added the CNDs solution to culture media along with NIH-3T3 cells and incubated for 30min. The washed cells were then imaged under bright field and UV excitation filter with a wavelength of 360nm. As observed from Fig. 4b and c, the cells incubated with CNDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence under UV excitation. The observation suggests that CNDs enter the cells and labeled cells could be imaged using a conventional fluorescence microscope. The CNDs were also evaluated for cytotoxic activity using an MTT assay (Fig. S1). The particles showed no cytotoxic effect on NIH-3T3 cells. Even at the highest concentration (200μgmL−1), there was no significant variation in the cytomorphology of the treated cells when compared to the control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The establishment of ratiometric fluorescent sensing system based on ESIPT–FRET
ce:section.ce:para: QCT is well known reagents for determination of Al3+ traces in biological samples (Fig. S5), and it possesses three possible chelating sites to complex with Al3+: the 3-hydroxychromone, the 5-hydroxychromone and the 3′4′-dihydroxyl groups. QCT gives rise to dual fluorescence with low quantum yield facilitates ESIPT process from the 3-hydroxy group to the neighboring carbonyl group of the chromone ring [45–47]. The enhancement of the fluorescence signal upon chelation of QCT with Al3+ in ethanol solution is related to the inhibition of the ESIPT process, so the ESIPT–FRET system was established in the ethanol solution (Fig. S6). In neutral medium, the stoichiometry ratio of Al3+ and QCT was 1:2 and 2:1, respectively (Fig. S7) [48].

In order to demonstrate the possibility of CNDs/QCT-Al3+ ESIPT–FRET sensing system, the normalized emission and absorption spectra of CNDs, QCT and QCT-Al3+ conjugate were measured (Fig. 5 a). It is seen that by QCT itself is there no obviously emission and absorption peak above 400nm, but addition of Al3+ gave rise to a strong absorption at λ max =428nm. This absorption spectrum has heavy overlap with the fluorescence spectrum of the donor CNDs at λ max =429nm, which is very important for the occurrence of a successful resonance energy transfer. This clearly indicates that excitation energy transfer could take place between CNDs and QCT-Al3+ conjugate to give fluorescent emission at λ max =481nm. Moreover, QCT-Al3+ conjugate is an ideal acceptor with a large extinction coefficient of 2140M−1 cm−1, which could bring a high efficient resonance energy transfer.

For further investigate the immobilization of CNDs and QCT-Al3+ conjugate, the zeta-potential measurements were carried out. Table 1 showed that both of CNDs and CNDs/QCT have negative surface potentials. The negative charge density declined and a positive surface potential obtained along with the addition of Al3+. This result suggests that both of QCT and QCT-Al3+ conjugate would effectively coordinate to the surface of the particle. Hydrogen bonding may contribute to the complexation of CNDs and QCT, and with the Al3+ was added, electrostatic attraction played a more important role. The distance between the donor nanoparticle and acceptor conjugate is not a fixed value and cannot be accurately determined, and one donor can interact with multiple acceptors residing in close proximity simultaneously. According to the Förster nonradiative energy transfer theory, Förster radius R 0, the critical distance between donor and acceptor at a transfer efficiency of 50%, is expressed as follows Eq. (1) in angstroms (Å):
(1)
R
0
= 0.2108 ×
K
2
×
Φ
D
×
n
R
- 4
× J ( λ )
1 / 6
K 2 is the orientation factor for the emission and absorption dipoles and its value depends on their relative orientation; nR is the refractive index of the medium, and ΦD is the quantum yield of the donor. J(λ) is the overlap integral of the fluorescence emission spectrum of the donor and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor, as expressed by the following Eq. (2):
(2)
J ( λ ) =
∫
0
∞
F
D
( λ ) ×
ε
A
( λ ) ×
λ
4
× d λ
FD (λ) is the normalized fluorescence intensity of the donor in the absence of acceptor; εA (λ) is molar extinction coefficient of the acceptor, and λ is wavelength. In the current experimental conditions, J(λ) was calculated to be 4.84×1015 M−1 cm−1 nm4. R 0 has been calculated as 64.20Å, assuming random orientation of the donor and acceptor moieties taking K 2 =2/3, nR
=1.366 (ethanol), and ΦD
=0.86.

To further demonstrate the existence of FRET, the FRET efficiency was calculated using the Eq. (3):
(3)
E = 1 -
F
DA
F
D
where FDA is the integrated fluorescence intensity of donor in presence of acceptor and FD is in absence of acceptors, and the FRET efficiency is calculated as 66.4%. The ratio of CNDs:QCT (μgmL−1:μM) with highest FRET efficiency was obtained between 1:10 and 1:4, and the ratio of 1:5 was used in this work (Fig. S8).

The CNDs/QCT-Al3+ based ESIPT–FRET ratiometric fluorescence system has been established: CNDs is chosen as FRET donor to a fluorescent QCT-Al3+ conjugate, the acceptor is formed by conjugating Al3+ with the non-fluorescent QCT. This approach can be a powerful tool for the detection of Al3+. We measured the fluorescence changes of the ESIPT–FRET system upon addition of Al3+ and established a working curve by plotting the ratio of the emission intensities at 429 and 481nm (F 429/F 481) versus Al3+ concentration, as shown in Fig. 5b and c. At low Al3+ concentration, the fluorescence of CNDs decreases due to enhanced absorbance around 428nm and energy transfer. At higher Al3+ concentration, the increase of resonance energy transfer efficiency causes a remarkable increment for the QCT-Al3+ conjugate fluorescence. There is no clear isosbestic point because emission peaks of donor and acceptor with a wide full width at half maximum (FWHM) are too close to separate completely. Note that Al3+ cannot quench the fluorescence of CNDs, indicating the changes of fluorescence caused by FRET process (Fig. S9). The ratio of fluorescence intensity at the two maximum wavelengths, i.e., F 429/F 481, correlates closely to Al3+ concentration, providing the basis for quantitative analysis. In the range of 0–20 and 20–60μM Al3+, the relative fluorescent intensity (F 429/F 481) could be described by linear equations with a perfect linear behavior. (F 429/F 481 =−0.13365C+3.24372 (R 2 =0.996); F 429/F 481 =−0.00516C+0.65299 (R 2 =0.964)) Herein, we calculated the detection limit of 558nM, which suggest that this ESIPT–FRET system has very promising application in the detection of Al3+.

In order to verify the practical applicability of the CNDs/QCT-Al3+ ESIPT–FRET ratiometric fluorescence sensing system, potential interferences from various metal cations were investigated by conducting selectivity trials and competition experiments. The sensing system was excited at 360nm and the fluorescence ratios of F 429/F 481 for the competing ions were recorded. Fig. 6 a and b show that changes in the fluorescence intensity of this ESIPT–FRET ratiometric fluorescence sensing system occurred with representative metal ions, including Al3+, Na+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Pb2+, K+, La3+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Ba2+ and Zn2+ respectively, with the same concentration of 20μM. Compare with other metal ions, 20μM of Al3+ caused unique changes of the fluorescence emission, i.e. enhancement and blue-shift of the emission peak. Many cation caused fluorescence quenching in 429nm, and Zn2+ and Mn2+ give rise to a new emission peak at 475 and 564nm respectively. However, they obviously have little significance for the detection of Al3+. The effects of the coexisting ions on Al3+ detection were also investigated (Fig. 6c). The result suggests that the coexistence of most selected metal ions does not interfere with the complexation of Al3+ with the QCT and the subsequent fluorescence enhancement and blue-shift.

The CNDs/QCT-Al3+ ESIPT–FRET ratiometric fluorescence sensing system was also investigated to prepare the fluorescent test paper by immersing filter papers into solution of 2μgmL−1 CNDs and 10μM QCT. The test papers were utilized to sense different concentrations of Al3+ including 0, 5, 10, 20 30 and 40μM. The L ∗ a ∗ b ∗ values have been used exclusively in the ratiometric fluorescence sensing owing to their popularity in virtually all fields of color measurement [49]. The L ∗ value ranges from 0 to 100 and increases with the lightness of the color. The +a ∗ and −a ∗ indicate the red and green directions, respectively, and the +b ∗ and −b ∗ point to the directions of yellow and blue colors, respectively. With the coordinates, the L ∗ a ∗ b ∗ system allows the numeric definition of a color as well as the difference between two colors. The color difference is calculated using the following equation:
Δ
E
ab
=
[
( Δ
L
∗
)
2
+
( Δ
a
∗
)
2
+
( Δ
b
∗
)
2
]
1 / 2

The Color parameters of the ESIPT–FRET system upon addition of Al3+ are shown in the Table 2 . With the increase in the concentration of Al3+, a ∗ value increased, b ∗ value decreased, while L ∗ value decreased and then increased. h value, the abbreviation of hue, increased from 223.0 to 263.1. This result is consistent with the fluorescence spectroscopy of the ESIPT–FRET system, and verifies that chromaticity can be used to determine the concentration of Al3+. In addition, to investigate the selectivity of the paper-based detection, fluorescent test papers were utilized to sense representative metal ions, including Fe3+, Cd2+, La3+ and Zn2+ respectively. As shown in Table S3, interfering ions caused a slight decrease of the lightness of the color (L ∗), but had little change on chromaticity (a ∗, b ∗ and h). Clearly, these potentially interfering metal ions do not interfere with this paper-based detection.

FRET system, that can be reversibly photoswitched, is particularly desirable because they can distinguish sites of interest from false positive signals generated by adventitious fluorescent substances. As above mentioned, we successfully applied CNDs as fluorescence ink for screen printing. To expand the CNDs patterns beyond a single fluorescent color in the patterns, we tuned the fluorescent emissions by doping CNDs with different concentrations of QCT and QCT-Al3+ (Fig. 7 ). The combination of CNDs with an appropriate fluorescent material allows for the manipulation of photoluminescence for multicolor patterns.

Novel carbon nanodots with strong fluorescence were prepared, and the fluorescence quantum yield is as high as 86%, which can be attributed to the doping of hetero atoms. The nitrogen-doping introduces a new kind of surface state. Electrons trapped by the new formed surface states are able to promote a high yield of radiative recombination. Due to the effective coordination, the high extent of spectral overlap, and most importantly, the proper distance between the donor and acceptor, a ratiometric sensor based on FRET from CNDs to QCT-Al3+ has the features for sensitive and selective measurement of Al3+ as well as its visual detection by colorimetric imaging. In addition, by performing measurement with a two-wavelength mode at the maximum fluorescent wavelength of CNDs and QCT-Al3+ conjugate, the spurious factors and the fluorescence fluctuations are effectively eliminated, and a wider linear range (1–20, 20–60μM) and a low a detection (558nM) were obtained.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this manuscript was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21174103, 21374078 and 31200719).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2014.06.056.
